--- SOURCE 2: Sustainable agriculture - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainable_agriculture

SUMMARY:
Contents Sustainable agriculture Sustainable agriculture is farming in sustainable ways meeting society's present food and textile needs, without compromising the ability for current or future generations to meet their needs. [6] Sustainable agriculture consists of environment friendly methods of farming that allow the production of crops or livestock without causing damage to human or natural systems. It involves preventing adverse effects on soil, water, biodiversity, and surrounding or downstream resources, as well as to those working or living on the farm or in neighboring areas. Sustainable agriculture provides a potential solution to enable agricultural systems to feed a growing population within the changing environmental conditions.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Sustainable agriculture

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Coffee_farm_in_Colombia.jpg/250px-Coffee_farm_in_Colombia.jpg)

**Sustainable agriculture** is [farming](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") in [sustainable](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") ways meeting society's present food and textile needs, without compromising the ability for current or future generations to meet their needs.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It can be based on an understanding of [ecosystem services](/wiki/Ecosystem_services "Ecosystem services"). There are many methods to increase the sustainability of agriculture. When developing agriculture within [sustainable food systems](/wiki/Sustainable_food_systems "Sustainable food systems"), it is important to develop flexible business processes and farming practices.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Agriculture has an enormous [environmental footprint](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_agriculture "Environmental impact of agriculture"), playing a significant role [in causing climate change](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions_from_agriculture "Greenhouse gas emissions from agriculture") ([food systems](/wiki/Food_system "Food system") are responsible for one third of the anthropogenic [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions")),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) [water scarcity](/wiki/Water_scarcity "Water scarcity"), [water pollution](/wiki/Water_pollution "Water pollution"), [land degradation](/wiki/Land_degradation "Land degradation"), [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") and other processes;[[5]](#cite_note-5) it is simultaneously causing environmental changes and being impacted by these changes.[[6]](#cite_note-Rockström-2016-6) Sustainable agriculture consists of [environment friendly](/wiki/Environment_friendly "Environment friendly") methods of farming that allow the production of crops or livestock without causing damage to human or natural systems. It involves preventing adverse effects on soil, water, biodiversity, and surrounding or downstream resources, as well as to those working or living on the farm or in neighboring areas. Elements of sustainable agriculture can include [permaculture](/wiki/Permaculture "Permaculture"), [agroforestry](/wiki/Agroforestry "Agroforestry"), [mixed farming](/wiki/Mixed_farming "Mixed farming"), [multiple cropping](/wiki/Multiple_cropping "Multiple cropping"), and [crop rotation](/wiki/Crop_rotation "Crop rotation").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Developing sustainable food systems contributes to the sustainability of the human population. For example, one of the best ways to mitigate climate change is to create sustainable food systems based on sustainable agriculture. Sustainable agriculture provides a potential solution to enable agricultural systems to feed a growing population within the changing environmental conditions.[[6]](#cite_note-Rockström-2016-6) Besides sustainable farming practices, [dietary shifts to sustainable diets](/wiki/Sustainable_diet "Sustainable diet") are an intertwined way to substantially reduce environmental impacts.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Numerous [sustainability standards and certification](/wiki/Sustainability_standards_and_certification "Sustainability standards and certification") systems exist, including [organic certification](/wiki/Organic_certification "Organic certification"), [Rainforest Alliance](/wiki/Rainforest_Alliance "Rainforest Alliance"), [Fair Trade](/wiki/Fairtrade_certification "Fairtrade certification"), [UTZ Certified](/wiki/UTZ_Certified "UTZ Certified"), [GlobalGAP](/wiki/GLOBALG.A.P "GLOBALG.A.P"), Bird Friendly, and the Common Code for the Coffee Community (4C).[[12]](#cite_note-SAI-12)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Maler_der_Grabkammer_des_Sennudem_001.jpg/250px-Maler_der_Grabkammer_des_Sennudem_001.jpg)
![grain](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Noto_Emoji_Oreo_1f33e.svg/20px-Noto_Emoji_Oreo_1f33e.svg.png)

The term "sustainable agriculture" was defined in 1977 by the [USDA](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture") as an integrated system of plant and animal production practices having a site-specific application that will, over the long term:[[13]](#cite_note-NARETPA-13)

Yet the idea of having a sustainable relationship with the land has been prevalent in indigenous communities for centuries before the term was formally added to the lexicon.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Aims

A common consensus is that sustainable farming is the most realistic way to feed growing populations. In order to successfully feed the population of the planet, farming practices must consider future costs–to both the environment and the communities they fuel.[[15]](#cite_note-Ehrlich-15) The risk of not being able to provide enough resources for everyone led to the adoption of technology within the sustainability field to increase farm productivity. The ideal end result of this advancement is the ability to feed ever-growing populations across the world. The growing popularity of sustainable agriculture is connected to the wide-reaching fear that the planet's [carrying capacity](/wiki/Carrying_capacity "Carrying capacity") (or [planetary boundaries](/wiki/Planetary_boundaries "Planetary boundaries")), in terms of the ability to feed humanity, has been reached or even exceeded.[[16]](#cite_note-Singh-2017-16)

### Key principles

There are several key principles associated with sustainability in agriculture:[[17]](#cite_note-Pretty2008-17)

It "considers long-term as well as short-term economics because sustainability is readily defined as forever, that is, agricultural environments that are designed to promote endless regeneration".[[18]](#cite_note-18) It balances the need for resource conservation with the needs of [farmers](/wiki/Farmer "Farmer") pursuing their [livelihood](/wiki/Livelihood "Livelihood").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

It is considered to be [reconciliation ecology](/wiki/Reconciliation_ecology "Reconciliation ecology"), accommodating biodiversity within human landscapes.[[20]](#cite_note-Chrispeels_1994-20)

Oftentimes, the execution of sustainable practices within farming comes through the adoption of technology and environmentally-focused [appropriate technology](/wiki/Appropriate_technology "Appropriate technology").

## Technological approaches

Sustainable agricultural systems are becoming an increasingly important field for AI research and development. By leveraging AI's skills in areas such as resource optimization, crop health monitoring, and yield prediction, farmers might greatly advance toward more environmentally friendly agricultural practices. AI-controlled irrigation systems optimize water consumption by using sensors to monitor soil moisture levels and weather conditions to distribute water accordingly. This water management technology can lower water consumption up to 30%.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Artificial intelligence (AI) mobile soil analysis enables farmers to enhance soil fertility while decreasing their ecological footprint. This technology permits on-site, real-time evaluations of soil nutrient levels.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Agrivoltaics enhances sustainable agriculture by optimizing land use—allowing crops to be grown alongside solar panels, which generate clean energy. This dual-use approach conserves land resources, improves microclimates, and can promote more resilient, eco-friendly farming practices.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Environmental factors

Practices that can cause long-term damage to [soil](/wiki/Soil "Soil") include excessive [tilling](/wiki/Tillage "Tillage") of the soil (leading to [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion")) and [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") without adequate drainage (leading to [salinization](/wiki/Soil_salinity "Soil salinity")).[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Conservation_farming_02.jpg/250px-Conservation_farming_02.jpg)

The most important factors for a farming site are [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate"), soil, [nutrients](/wiki/Nutrient "Nutrient") and [water resources](/wiki/Water_resources "Water resources"). Of the four, water and [soil conservation](/wiki/Soil_conservation "Soil conservation") are the most amenable to human intervention. When farmers grow and harvest crops, they remove some nutrients from the soil. Without replenishment, the land suffers from [nutrient depletion](/wiki/Nutrient_depletion "Nutrient depletion") and becomes either unusable or suffers from reduced [yields](/wiki/Crop_yield "Crop yield"). Sustainable agriculture depends on replenishing the soil while minimizing the use or need of non-renewable resources, such as [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") or mineral ores.

A farm that can "produce perpetually", yet has negative effects on environmental quality elsewhere is not sustainable agriculture. An example of a case in which a global view may be warranted is the application of [fertilizer](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer") or [manure](/wiki/Manure "Manure"), which can improve the productivity of a farm but can pollute nearby rivers and coastal waters ([eutrophication](/wiki/Eutrophication "Eutrophication")).[[26]](#cite_note-26) The other extreme can also be undesirable, as the problem of low crop yields due to exhaustion of nutrients in the soil has been related to [rainforest](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest") destruction.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In Asia, the specific amount of land needed for sustainable farming is about 12.5 acres (5.1 ha) which include land for animal fodder, cereal production as a cash crop, and other food crops. In some cases, a small unit of aquaculture is included (AARI-1996).

### Nutrients

#### Nitrates

Nitrates are used widely in farming as fertilizer. Unfortunately, a major environmental problem associated with agriculture is the leaching of nitrates into the environment.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Possible sources of [nitrates](/wiki/Nitrate "Nitrate") that would, in principle, be available indefinitely, include:

The last option was proposed in the 1970s, but is only gradually becoming feasible.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Sustainable options for replacing other nutrient inputs such as phosphorus and potassium are more limited.

Other options include [long-term crop rotations](/wiki/Shifting_cultivation "Shifting cultivation"), returning to natural cycles that annually flood cultivated lands (returning lost nutrients) such as the [flooding of the Nile](/wiki/Flooding_of_the_Nile "Flooding of the Nile"), the long-term use of [biochar](/wiki/Biochar "Biochar"), and use of crop and livestock [landraces](/wiki/Landrace "Landrace") that are adapted to less than ideal conditions such as pests, drought, or lack of nutrients. Crops that require high levels of soil nutrients can be cultivated in a more sustainable manner with appropriate fertilizer management practices.

#### Phosphate

[Phosphate](/wiki/Phosphate "Phosphate") is a primary component in [fertilizer](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer"). It is the second most important nutrient for plants after nitrogen,[[34]](#cite_note-Atekan-2014-34) and is often a limiting factor.[[35]](#cite_note-Khan-2007-35) It is important for sustainable agriculture as it can improve soil fertility and crop yields.[[36]](#cite_note-Cordell-2013-36) Phosphorus is involved in all major metabolic processes including photosynthesis, energy transfer, signal transduction, macromolecular biosynthesis, and respiration. It is needed for root ramification and strength and seed formation, and can increase disease resistance.[[37]](#cite_note-Sharma-2013-37)

Phosphorus is found in the soil in both inorganic and organic forms[[34]](#cite_note-Atekan-2014-34) and makes up approximately 0.05% of soil biomass.[[37]](#cite_note-Sharma-2013-37) Phosphorus fertilizers are the main input of inorganic phosphorus in agricultural soils and approximately 70%–80% of phosphorus in cultivated soils is inorganic.[[38]](#cite_note-Bhattacharya2019-38) Long-term use of phosphate-containing chemical fertilizers causes [eutrophication](/wiki/Eutrophication "Eutrophication") and deplete soil microbial life, so people have looked to other sources.[[37]](#cite_note-Sharma-2013-37)

Phosphorus fertilizers are manufactured from [rock phosphate](/wiki/Rock_phosphate#uses "Rock phosphate").[[39]](#cite_note-39) However, rock phosphate is a non-renewable resource and it is being depleted by mining for agricultural use:[[36]](#cite_note-Cordell-2013-36)[[38]](#cite_note-Bhattacharya2019-38) [peak phosphorus](/wiki/Peak_phosphorus "Peak phosphorus") will occur within the next few hundred years,[[40]](#cite_note-ifdc.org-40)[[41]](#cite_note-USGS2017-41)[[42]](#cite_note-VanKauwenbergh-42) or perhaps earlier.[[43]](#cite_note-Edixhoven2013-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

#### Potassium

Potassium is a macronutrient very important for plant development and is commonly sought in fertilizers.[[46]](#cite_note-46) This nutrient is essential for agriculture because it improves water retention, nutrient value, yield, taste, color, texture and disease resistance of crops. It is often used in the cultivation of grains, fruits, vegetables, rice, wheat, millets, sugar, corn, soybeans, [palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil "Palm oil") and coffee.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Potassium chloride (KCl) represents the most widely source of K used in agriculture,[[48]](#cite_note-48) accounting for 90% of all potassium produced for agricultural use.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

The use of KCl leads to high concentrations of chloride (Clˉ) in soil harming its health due to the increase in soil salinity, imbalance in nutrient availability and this ion's biocidal effect for soil organisms.[[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0098847218309699#:~:text=An%20overabundance%20of%20Cl%E2%88%92%20in,contaminated%20soils%20should%20be%20restored.) In consequences the development of plants and soil organisms is affected, putting at risk [soil biodiversity](/wiki/Soil_biodiversity "Soil biodiversity") and agricultural productivity.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) A sustainable option for replacing KCl are chloride-free fertilizers, its use should take into account plants' nutrition needs, and the promotion of soil health.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Soil

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Walls_against_water_runoff.JPG/250px-Walls_against_water_runoff.JPG)

[Land degradation](/wiki/Land_degradation "Land degradation") is becoming a severe global problem. According to the [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](/wiki/Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change"): "About a quarter of the Earth's ice-free land area is subject to human-induced degradation (medium confidence). Soil erosion from agricultural fields is estimated to be currently 10 to 20 times (no tillage) to more than 100 times (conventional tillage) higher than the soil formation rate (medium confidence)."[[56]](#cite_note-56) Almost half of the land on earth is covered with dry land, which is susceptible to degradation.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Over a billion tonnes of southern Africa's soil are being lost to erosion annually, which if continued will result in halving of crop yields within thirty to fifty years.[[58]](#cite_note-58) A comparative study of two adjacent wheat farms—one using sustainable practices and the other conventional methods—found that the sustainable farm had significantly better soil quality, including higher organic matter, microbial populations, and nutrient content, while also showing 22.4% higher net returns due to lower input costs, despite slightly lower yields.[[59]](#cite_note-59)
Improper [soil management](/wiki/Soil_management "Soil management") is threatening the ability to grow sufficient food. [Intensive agriculture](/wiki/Intensive_agriculture "Intensive agriculture") reduces the [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") level in soil, impairing soil structure, crop growth and ecosystem functioning,[[60]](#cite_note-Powlson-2011-60) and accelerating [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[60]](#cite_note-Powlson-2011-60) Modification of agricultural practices is a recognized method of [carbon sequestration](/wiki/Carbon_sequestration "Carbon sequestration") as soil can act as an effective [carbon sink](/wiki/Carbon_sink "Carbon sink").[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Soil management techniques include [no-till farming](/wiki/No-till_farming "No-till farming"), [keyline design](/wiki/Keyline_design "Keyline design") and [windbreaks](/wiki/Windbreaks "Windbreaks") to reduce wind erosion, [reincorporation of organic matter](/wiki/Compost "Compost") into the soil, reducing [soil salinization](/wiki/Soil_salinity "Soil salinity"), and preventing water run-off.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Land

As the global population increases and demand for food increases, there is pressure on land as a resource. In [land-use planning](/wiki/Land-use_planning "Land-use planning") and management, considering the impacts of [land-use changes](/wiki/Land-use_change "Land-use change") on factors such as soil erosion can support long-term agricultural sustainability, as shown by a study of Wadi Ziqlab, a dry area in the Middle East where farmers graze livestock and grow olives, vegetables, and grains.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Looking back over the 20th century shows that for people in poverty, following environmentally sound land practices has not always been a viable option due to many complex and challenging life circumstances.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Currently, increased [land degradation](/wiki/Land_degradation "Land degradation") in developing countries may be connected with rural poverty among smallholder farmers when forced into unsustainable agricultural practices out of necessity.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Converting big parts of the land surface to agriculture has severe environmental and health consequences. For example, it leads to rise in [zoonotic disease](/wiki/Zoonotic_disease "Zoonotic disease") (like the [Coronavirus disease 2019](/wiki/Coronavirus_disease_2019 "Coronavirus disease 2019")) due to the degradation of natural buffers between humans and animals, reducing biodiversity and creating larger groups of genetically similar animals.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

Land is a finite resource on Earth. Although expansion of agricultural land can decrease [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") and contribute to [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), the picture is complex; for instance, a study examining the introduction of sheep by Norse settlers (Vikings) to the Faroe Islands of the North Atlantic concluded that, over time, the fine partitioning of land plots contributed more to soil erosion and degradation than grazing itself.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") of the United Nations estimates that in coming decades, cropland will continue to be lost to industrial and [urban development](/wiki/Urban_development "Urban development"), along with reclamation of wetlands, and conversion of forest to cultivation, resulting in the [loss of biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity_loss "Biodiversity loss") and increased soil erosion.[[70]](#cite_note-fao2008-70)

### Energy

In modern agriculture, energy is used in on-farm mechanisation, food processing, storage, and transportation processes.[[71]](#cite_note-fao2013-71) It has therefore been found that energy prices are closely linked to [food prices](/wiki/Food_prices "Food prices").[[72]](#cite_note-fao-72) Oil is also used as an input in [agricultural chemicals](/wiki/Agrochemical "Agrochemical"). The [International Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Energy_Agency "International Energy Agency") projects higher prices of non-renewable energy resources as a result of fossil fuel resources being depleted. It may therefore decrease global [food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security") unless action is taken to 'decouple' fossil fuel energy from food production, with a move towards 'energy-smart' agricultural systems including [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy").[[72]](#cite_note-fao-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)
The use of solar powered irrigation in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") is said to be a closed system for agricultural water irrigation.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The environmental cost of transportation could be avoided

Agriculture and Sustainability
==============================

Sustainability is based on a simple principle: Everything that we need for our survival and well-being depends, either directly or indirectly, on our natural environment. Agriculture contributes directly to sustainable stewardship of the land, air and water. Sustainability creates and maintains the conditions under which humans and nature can exist in productive harmony, that permit fulfilling the food, feed, and fiber needs of our country and the social, economic and other requirements of present and future generations.

Related Information

*   [Ag 101](https://www.epa.gov/agriculture/ag-101-overview-agriculture)
*   [Agriculture: Working with the Agricultural Community to Protect Land, Air and Water Resources](https://www.epa.gov/agriculture/agriculture-working-agricultural-community-protect-land-air-and-water-resources)

Sustainable agriculture is important to making sure that we have and will continue to have, the water, materials, and resources to protect human health and our environment.

-----------------------------------

*   [AgStar](https://www.epa.gov/agstar)

*   [Smart Growth](https://www.epa.gov/smartgrowth)- Smart growth helps communities grow in ways that expand economic opportunity while protecting human health and the environment.
*   [Local Food, Local Places](https://www.epa.gov/smartgrowth/local-foods-local-places)- Local Foods, Local Places helps communities create walkable, healthy, economically vibrant neighborhoods through the development of local food systems.

Sustainability Information from Other Sources
---------------------------------------------

*   [USDA Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education (SARE)](http://www.sare.org/)- Since 1988, the SARE grants and education program has advanced agricultural innovation that promotes profitability, stewardship of the land, air and water, and quality of life for farmers, ranchers and their communities.

Sustainable Agriculture and Tribes
----------------------------------

*   [People of the Land Publication: Sustaining American Indian Agriculture in Idaho, Nevada, Oregon and Washington](http://www.sare.org/Learning-Center/SARE-Project-Products/Western-SARE-Project-Products/People-of-the-Land)--A curriculum from the Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education program aims to help those who work with American Indians better understand the unique challenges faced by native farmers. The materials are titled "People of the Land".

Sustainable Management of Food
------------------------------

[Sustainable Management of Food](https://www.epa.gov/sustainable-management-food) is a systematic approach that seeks to reduce wasted food and its associated impacts over the entire life cycle, starting with the use of natural resources, manufacturing, sales, and consumption and ending with decisions on recovery or final disposal. EPA works to promote innovation and highlight the value and efficient management of food as a resource. Through the sustainable management of food, we can help businesses and consumers save money, provide a bridge in our communities for those who do not have enough to eat, and conserve resources for future generations. Building on the familiar concept of "Reduce, Reuse, Recycle," this approach shifts the view on environmental protection and more fully recognizes the impacts of the food we waste.

### Related Publications

*   [Case Study: New Seasons Market Feeding the Soil and those in Need](https://nepis.epa.gov/Exe/ZyPURL.cgi?Dockey=P100MWT4.txt) -- Case study on the environmental and human health impacts recycling food wastes has on soil quality and leads to more sustainable agriculture in addition to saving lots of money.
*   [Anaerobic Digestion and its Applications](https://nepis.epa.gov/Exe/ZyPURL.cgi?Dockey=P100NPK0.txt) -- Report on how AD can help generate "green" soils and minimize environmental impacts using sustainable agriculture.
*   [Reducing Wasted Food and Packaging: A Guide for Food Services and Restaurants](https://nepis.epa.gov/Exe/ZyPURL.cgi?Dockey=P100I7UW.txt) -- Guide on methods to divert food scraps and waste to feed people, create healthy soils, create energy, and to be used as animal feed on farms.
*   [Food Scraps Go to the Animals: Barthold Recycling and Roll-off Services](https://nepis.epa.gov/Exe/ZyPURL.cgi?Dockey=P10009P9.txt)-- Factsheet/advertisement for a company that picks up food waste and delivers it to farms to be used as animal feed.

--- SOURCE 5: Sustainable Agriculture - USDA NIFA ---
URL: https://www.nifa.usda.gov/topics/sustainable-agriculture

SUMMARY:
Sustainable agricultural practices are intended to protect the environment, expand the Earth's natural resource base, and maintain and improve soil fertility.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://www.greenlivinganswers.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/sustainable-agriculture.webp
DESCRIPTION: A vibrant agricultural landscape features rows of crops, a farmer, a tractor, wind turbines, and solar panels, illustrating sustainable farming practices.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://sdgresources.relx.com/sites/default/files/styles/sus_content_main_image/public/1-s2.0-s2590332221004735-fx1_lrg.jpg?itok=BWwcCeQF
DESCRIPTION: The diagram outlines the Sustainable Agriculture Matrix, highlighting key elements across economic, social, and environmental categories that influence agricultural development pathways in both developed and developing countries.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://www.bivatec.com/storage/canvas/images/PPtlPjXdsTRusXC3jcdm4XgBrXXxiu2anqR8dpFH.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A pair of hands gently holds soil with a small plant sprouting from it, accompanied by points highlighting the benefits of sustainable farming for small-scale farmers.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Immediate action is vital to protect ecosystems, mitigate climate change, and ensure resources for future generations. Sustainable practices are essential for our planet's health.

enriched_points.point_title: Impact of Agriculture on Climate Change
enriched_points.expanded_content: Agriculture contributes significantly to greenhouse gas emissions, leading to climate change. Sustainable farming can reduce this impact and promote environmental restoration.
Sustainable farming enhances soil health, conserves water, and promotes biodiversity. It ensures food security while minimizing harm to the environment and human health.

enriched_points.point_title: Role of Policy Incentives in Agriculture
enriched_points.expanded_content: Policy incentives support farmers in adopting sustainable practices, providing financial assistance and educational resources. They are crucial for transitioning to environmentally friendly agriculture.
